name: Agentic AI Workflow
description: Multi-step autonomous AI agent that plans, reasons, and executes tasks using tools and APIs. Includes a planning loop, tool execution, memory/state, and human-in-the-loop checkpoints. The agent decomposes complex goals into subtasks, selects appropriate tools, executes actions against external systems, and iterates until the objective is met or escalation is triggered.
logisticsContext: Agentic workflows handle complex logistics operations like multi-leg shipment booking, carrier selection and negotiation, customs documentation assembly, and exception management. They interact with TMS, booking APIs, carrier portals, and payment systems.

nodes.label: User / Trigger Interface
nodes.description: Entry point for agent tasks â€” can be a human request via chat or dashboard, an automated API call, a scheduled cron trigger, or an event-driven webhook. All task inputs enter the system here, including natural language instructions, structured payloads, and follow-up corrections. This is the primary injection surface for adversarial task descriptions.
vulnerabilities: Direct prompt injection via crafted task descriptions designed to override agent constraints and execute unauthorized actions
Automated trigger abuse flooding the agent with high-volume tasks to exhaust API quotas or model tokens
Task payload tampering injecting hidden instructions in structured fields (e.g., shipment notes containing override commands)
Session replay attacks resubmitting previously approved tasks with modified parameters

nodes.label: Agent Orchestrator
nodes.description: Central coordination loop that manages the agent lifecycle: receives tasks, invokes the planning module, dispatches tool calls, evaluates results, and decides whether to continue, retry, or escalate. Maintains the execution state machine and enforces step limits to prevent infinite loops. This is the most privileged component in the architecture.
vulnerabilities: Infinite loop exploitation where adversarial task descriptions cause the orchestrator to retry indefinitely, exhausting compute and API budgets
Step limit bypass through task descriptions that instruct the agent to reset its step counter or ignore iteration bounds
State confusion from concurrent task execution causing the orchestrator to mix context between unrelated tasks
Privilege escalation if the orchestrator's internal control flow can be influenced by LLM-generated reasoning traces

nodes.label: Planning Module
nodes.description: Decomposes high-level task objectives into ordered subtask sequences. Uses the LLM to reason about task dependencies, identify required tools, estimate step count, and produce an execution plan. Re-plans dynamically when tool calls fail or return unexpected results. Plans are validated against the policy engine before execution begins.
vulnerabilities: Plan manipulation through adversarial task descriptions that steer the planner toward dangerous tool sequences (e.g., delete before backup)
Dependency injection where crafted inputs cause the planner to include unauthorized tools in the execution plan
Over-planning attacks generating excessively complex plans that consume maximum tokens and compute before any action is taken
Goal drift when iterative re-planning gradually shifts the objective away from the original user intent

nodes.label: Memory / State Store
nodes.description: Persists conversation history, task progress, intermediate results, and agent context across planning and execution steps. Stores both short-term working memory (current task state) and long-term memory (past task outcomes, learned preferences). Feeds context back into the LLM for informed decision-making. Contains sensitive operational data including booking references, rate agreements, and customer identifiers.
vulnerabilities: Memory poisoning where injected content in past conversation turns influences future agent decisions and tool selections
Context window overflow attacks stuffing memory with verbose data to push critical instructions out of the LLM context window
Sensitive data accumulation as memory retains booking references, rate agreements, and customer PII across sessions without expiration
Cross-task contamination when memory from one customer's task leaks into another customer's agent context

nodes.label: Tool Registry
nodes.description: Catalog of all tools, APIs, and functions available to the agent, including their schemas, required parameters, permission levels, and rate limits. The LLM references this registry to select appropriate tools for each subtask. Controls the agent's capability surface â€” any tool registered here can potentially be invoked by the agent.
vulnerabilities: Excessive agency through overly broad tool registration granting the agent access to dangerous operations (delete, transfer funds, modify permissions)
Schema poisoning if tool descriptions contain hidden instructions that influence the LLM's tool selection behavior
Missing permission granularity where tools lack per-action authorization, allowing the agent to perform any operation a tool exposes
Stale tool registrations pointing to deprecated or compromised API endpoints

nodes.label: Tool Executor
nodes.description: Runtime layer that actually executes tool calls against external systems. Marshals parameters from the LLM's structured output, authenticates against target APIs, handles retries and timeouts, and returns results to the orchestrator. Operates with real credentials and can trigger irreversible actions in external systems.
vulnerabilities: Credential exposure if API keys and service account tokens are accessible to the LLM through tool execution logs or error messages
Parameter injection where LLM-generated tool arguments contain malicious payloads passed directly to external APIs (SQL injection, command injection)
Irreversible action execution without pre-validation â€” booking confirmations, payment authorizations, or shipment cancellations executed before human review
Rate limit exhaustion through rapid sequential tool calls draining API quotas and incurring overage charges

nodes.label: External APIs / Systems
nodes.description: Third-party services the agent interacts with: TMS platforms, carrier booking APIs, customs declaration portals, payment gateways, ERP systems, email services, and document management systems. Each external system has its own authentication, rate limits, and data formats. Responses from these systems re-enter the agent loop and influence subsequent decisions.
vulnerabilities: Indirect prompt injection via external API responses containing adversarial content that manipulates agent behavior when parsed by the LLM
Data integrity compromise when carrier or TMS APIs return manipulated rate quotes, availability data, or shipment statuses
Man-in-the-middle attacks on API connections intercepting booking confirmations or payment authorizations
Supply chain risk from compromised third-party API SDKs or libraries used for integration

nodes.label: LLM / Foundation Model
nodes.description: Core reasoning engine that powers the agent's decision-making. Processes the system prompt, task context, memory, tool registry, and execution results to determine next actions. Generates structured tool call arguments, evaluates success criteria, and produces natural language explanations. May be cloud-hosted (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini) or self-hosted. In agentic patterns, the LLM operates in a loop with significantly more autonomy than in single-turn applications.
vulnerabilities: Prompt injection via task descriptions or tool results causing the model to deviate from system prompt constraints and execute unauthorized actions
Excessive autonomy when the model makes high-impact decisions (financial transactions, shipment routing) without sufficient guardrails
Hallucinated tool arguments generating plausible but incorrect API parameters (wrong booking reference, invalid customs code) that cause real-world errors
System prompt leakage revealing agent capabilities, tool schemas, and internal constraints to adversarial users
Unbounded token consumption in long-running agentic loops where each iteration adds to the context window
Reasoning chain manipulation where adversarial content in tool results steers the model's chain-of-thought toward attacker-desired outcomes

nodes.label: System Prompt / Agent Instructions
nodes.description: Defines the agent's role, behavioral constraints, permitted actions, prohibited operations, output format requirements, escalation rules, and safety guardrails. Contains domain-specific instructions (e.g., always verify customs codes, never auto-approve payments above threshold). This is the primary control mechanism for agent behavior â€” its integrity determines the agent's operational envelope.
vulnerabilities: Prompt leakage through adversarial task descriptions that trick the LLM into revealing system prompt contents including tool schemas and thresholds
Instruction override when injected content in tool results or memory supersedes system prompt constraints
Insufficient guardrails failing to constrain the agent's tool usage scope, allowing unintended operations
Hardcoded thresholds embedded in the prompt (e.g., approval limits) that become attack targets once leaked

nodes.label: Output Validator / Policy Engine
nodes.description: Validates all agent actions before execution against a policy ruleset. Checks tool call parameters for safety (amount limits, allowed destinations, permitted operations), validates output format, enforces business rules (e.g., maximum booking value without approval), and flags high-risk actions for human review. Acts as the guardrail between LLM decisions and real-world execution.
vulnerabilities: Policy bypass through LLM-crafted arguments that technically satisfy validation rules while violating their intent (e.g., splitting a large payment into multiple small ones)
Incomplete policy coverage where novel tool combinations or action sequences are not covered by existing rules
Race conditions where the validator approves an action based on state that changes before execution completes
False negatives in risk classification allowing high-impact actions to bypass human review

nodes.label: Human-in-the-Loop Gate
nodes.description: Approval checkpoint for high-risk agent actions. When the output validator flags an action (e.g., booking above threshold, payment authorization, customs submission, carrier contract modification), execution pauses and a human reviewer receives the proposed action with context. The reviewer can approve, reject, or modify the action before it proceeds to the tool executor.
vulnerabilities: Approval fatigue where high volume of low-risk approvals causes reviewers to rubber-stamp high-risk actions
Context manipulation where the agent presents misleading summaries that bias the reviewer toward approval
Timeout auto-approval if the system defaults to executing actions when human review is not completed within a window
Impersonation attacks where adversarial content mimics approval signals to bypass the human gate

nodes.label: Audit Logger
nodes.description: Immutable audit trail capturing every agent decision, tool call, API response, human approval, policy evaluation, and error event. Records the full reasoning chain including LLM inputs/outputs, tool parameters, and execution results. Essential for compliance, incident investigation, cost attribution, and detecting anomalous agent behavior patterns.
vulnerabilities: Log injection attacks embedding malicious payloads in task descriptions or tool results that execute when logs are viewed in dashboards
Sensitive data in logs if booking references, customer PII, rate agreements, or payment details are stored without redaction
Log tampering if the audit trail is not append-only, allowing post-hoc modification to cover unauthorized agent actions
Insufficient retention causing loss of audit records needed for compliance investigations or dispute resolution

nodes.label: Error Handler
nodes.description: Manages failures across the agent lifecycle: tool call timeouts, API errors, validation failures, rate limit hits, and unexpected LLM outputs. Implements retry logic with exponential backoff, fallback tool selection, graceful degradation, and escalation to human operators when automated recovery fails. Prevents the agent from entering undefined states after failures.
vulnerabilities: Error message information disclosure revealing internal API endpoints, credentials, or system architecture in error responses
Retry amplification where adversarial inputs trigger repeated retries against rate-limited APIs, amplifying cost and quota consumption
Fallback path exploitation where attackers intentionally trigger errors to force the agent into less-secured fallback execution paths
Escalation flooding where automated error escalation overwhelms human operators, creating a denial-of-service on the review queue

edges.label: Task request with instructions

API call logs with parameters and response codes
